model_summary: This initial gVAR-ready observation model targets Six principal criteria of appetite/eating regulation (C01–C06) and maps them to six modifiable predictors (P01–P06) spanning sleep/circadian alignment, structured meal planning, mindful eating, environmental nighttime access control, pre-sleep routines, and self-monitoring logs. The design emphasizes within-person variance via brief, frequent momentary assessments (4 prompts/day over 14 days) plus a simple daily diary to anchor sleep and mood context, while minimizing redundancy by using one broad sleep/circadian construct, one structured eating construct, and one cognitive-behavioral/self-regulation construct per domain. This enables clean estimation of directional and non-linear relationships through gVAR while preserving the complaint’s resolution into actionable levers.
variable_selection_notes: Criteria C01–C06 capture appetite lability, nocturnal eating, post-episode distress, dieting pressures, access to nighttime food, and circadian opportunities for eating. Predictors were chosen to cover biological (sleep, nutrition), psychological (cognitive-emotional regulation), and environmental/social domains with minimal overlap. Sampling design uses 4 EMA prompts/day to maximize within-person variance; all predictors and criteria use 1–9 Likert scales where applicable, except where counts are explicit (C02). The plan anticipates non-linear or unknown effects for some domains (e.g., nighttime food access behavior) by allowing non-monotonic expectations in the predicted effects and to flag potential nonlinearity in the edges/diagnostics. Anticipated data quality risks (missing prompts, misreporting) are mitigated with anchor diary (evening) and reminders.

momentary_ema.notes: Prompts cover all 6 criteria and 6 predictors per block; prompts may include multiple items.

daily_diary.notes: Evening diary for sleep, mood, and generalized intake reflections.

passive_streams.notes: No passive streams in this initial model.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Within-person variance expected across sleep, hunger, and nighttime eating cycles; plan to standardize within-subject (z-score) and inspect residual collinearity.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Expect variability across days and nights; use within-person scaling; consider hour-of-day effects.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Use reminders; allow brief catch-up prompts; implement reasonable time blocks to reduce missingness.

safety_notes: This is not a diagnosis or treatment plan. If acute risk factors arise, escalate to a licensed professional.

criteria_variables.label: Appetite lability (no hunger vs intense hunger)

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your current hunger level and appetite urge on a 1–9 scale (1 = no hunger, 9 = extreme hunger).

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / emotional / arousal / wanting / desire / *craving
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C01 (Appetite lability) is captured via EMA self-report of hunger/appetite urge on a 1–9 scale. The closest, most specific operational match among candidates is idx 5891 (craving) under NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / emotional / arousal / wanting / desire / craving, which directly represents the hunger/appetite urge intensity measured by the item. This leaf best aligns with the momentary hunger drive assessed by the EMA prompt in the pseudoprofile context.

The candidate leaf 26807 is 'loss_of_control_during_night_eating' within night eating syndrome / night eating disorder cluster (DSM-5TR). Measurement context (EMA, past 6 hours, 4 prompts/day) fits the operational Night eating episodes/urge intensity structure captured by the item, providing a specific operational construct for uncontrolled nocturnal intake. Other candidates are broader (nocturnal eating episodes) or focus on awareness/distress rather than loss of control; hence 26807 is the most specific operational leaf.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep Regularization and Circadian Alignment
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Aligns sleep with circadian rhythm to reduce nocturnal wakefulness and hunger fluctuations.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Set a consistent bed/wake time; limit evening light exposure; avoid caffeine after 6 pm.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate how consistent your sleep schedule was (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Regular sleep reduces daytime hunger variability.
Stabilized sleep reduces nocturnal eating episodes.
Better sleep may blunt post-eating negative affect.
Predictable schedule reduces tomorrow’s restriction pressure.
Circadian alignment lowers nighttime food accessibility cues.
Consistent sleep reduces late-night wakefulness.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf: Sleep circadian entrainment pathway with the specific leaf 'consistent_morning_anchor_routine' (consistent morning anchor routine). This directly maps to Sleep Regularization and Circadian Alignment by anchoring the sleep schedule to a stable morning routine, a key circadian entrainment cue. The measurement item assesses how consistent the sleep schedule was (1-9) via EMA, so a leaf emphasizing a consistent morning routine provides the most specific operational construct for P01 among the BIO circadian entrainment options, more so than broader leaves like general sleep diary or caffeine timing adjustments.

predictor_variables.label: Meal Planning and Structure
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Structured meals support satiety, reduce hunger swings and nocturnal eating.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Use meal templates; plan 3 meals + 1-2 planned snacks; grocery planning.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate how structured your meals were (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Planned meals stabilize appetite signals.
Structured meals reduce nocturnal episodes.
Regular meals may lessen post-eating distress.
Meal plan supports flexible dieting rather than all-or-nothing rules.
Structured eating reduces nighttime access cues.
Better structure supports earlier sleep onset.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable P02 corresponds to Meal Planning and Structure. The most specific matching leaf is recipe_rotation_system under meal_planning_and_structure -> planning_tools. This directly represents a structured planning tool for meals (planning tools) and aligns with the measurement item 'Today, rate how structured your meals were (1-9)' by capturing the degree of meal structure via a rotation system. It is the most precise operational construct among candidates, better than snack-focused or broader template tools, and fits the pseudoprofile’s emphasis on a single, coherent domain per area.

predictor_variables.label: Mindful Eating Practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Mindful awareness of hunger/fullness reduces impulsive intake and post-meal distress.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Practice brief mindful eating cues; eat slowly; check-in with hunger signals.

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your level of mindful eating (attention to hunger/fullness) today on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Mindful eating reduces post-eating disgust and guilt.
Increased mindfulness improves appetite regulation.
Awareness can reduce nighttime intake urges.
Mindful approach supports flexible restraint.
Mindfulness reduces cue-driven access to nighttime foods.
Calm awareness may improve sleep onset.

predictor_variables.label: Environmental Nighttime Access Reduction
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Limiting nighttime food accessibility reduces cues and temptation for late eating.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Remove easy nighttime snacks; use containerized snacks; keep kitchen door closed.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate how easy it was to access nighttime foods in your home (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Reduced access lowers nocturnal eating frequency.
Lower accessibility cues decrease nighttime intake.
Fewer opportunities support better sleep.
Reduced cues may stabilize appetite swings.
Structure and reduced temptations lessen tomorrow’s restriction pressure.
Less nighttime exposure reduces post-eating distress triggers.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable P04 targets reducing access to nighttime foods via the home environment. The EMA item directly assesses 'ease of access' to nighttime foods, reflecting environmental cues/availability. The closest, most specific ontology leaf is BIO / nutrition_hydration_and_metabolic_health / eating_behavior_and_satiety_skills / environment_design_for_eating, which encapsulates modifying the eating environment to influence intake and access at night. Other candidates focus on lighting or noise rather than food access, making environment_design_for_eating the best fit for the intended mechanism.

predictor_variables.label: Pre-Sleep Wind-Down and Screen Time Constraints
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: A calm pre-sleep window reduces sleep fragmentation and late-night wakefulness, indirectly moderating appetite cycles.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Limit screen time 60 minutes before bed; read a non-stimulating book; off-device winding-down activities.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate your pre-sleep wind-down quality (1-9) and screen exposure (0-1) in the last hour.

expected_effects.rationale: Better pre-sleep routine supports earlier and more stable sleep.
Reduced late-night eating opportunities via wind-down effects.
Stabilized hunger cues through consistent evening routines.
Less nighttime accessibility cues during wind-down.
Less promise-like restriction pressure due to calmer evenings.
Reduced arousal may blunt post-eating distress.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf is presleep_detachment_routine_design_psychological (PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / presleep_mental_skills / detachment_and_unloading). The variable P05 represents a psychological wind-down routine before sleep and constraints on screen time, aligning with presleep cognitive-emotional downregulation strategies. The measurement items (pre-sleep wind-down quality; last-hour screen exposure) map to cognitive/behavioral detachment and unloading processes intended to reduce pre-sleep arousal, which this leaf explicitly targets. It is the most specific PSYCHO node within Presleep_downshift that captures a structured, cognitive-behavioral wind-down design, consistent with the pseudoprofile's domain organization.

predictor_variables.label: Self-Monitoring and Appetite/Satiety Logs
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Frequent logging of appetite and satiety yields within-person variance and detects changes linked to C01–C06.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Simple appetite/satiety diary entries; timestamped notes on meals.

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate current appetite and satiety on a 1–9 scale; optionally log last meal size (small/medium/large).

expected_effects.rationale: Regular logging improves self-awareness of hunger fluctuations.
More complete episode accounting supports detection of nocturnal eating patterns.
Immediate logging may reduce rumination about post-eating distress.
Feedback loops help adjust tomorrow’s intake planning.
Reflective logging reduces cue-driven cravings.
Logs enable detection of sleep–hunger coupling.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: P06 maps to Self-Monitoring and Appetite/Satiety Logs. The best-matching leaf is appetite_and_satiety_log (idx 4511) under BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / somatic_and_interoceptive_selfreports. This leaf directly corresponds to logging appetite and satiety, which aligns with the variable label, and the item/mode details match: measurement.item_or_signal = 'Rate current appetite and satiety on a 1–9 scale; optionally log last meal size'; measurement.mode = EMA_self_report; measurement.channel = Smartphone EMA app; measurement.assessment_type = momentary; recall_window aligns with 'since last_prompt'. The mechanism rationale (frequent logging yields within-person variance to detect changes related to C01–C06) also fits. This leaf is the most specific operational construct for P06 and matches the intended data-collection design (4 EMA prompts/day).

source_variable.label: Appetite lability (no hunger vs intense hunger)

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your current hunger level and appetite urge on a 1–9 scale (1 = no hunger, 9 = extreme hunger).

criteria.label: Nighttime uncontrolled eating episodes
criteria.query_text_used: Nighttime uncontrolled eating episodes | Operational item/signal: In the past 6 hours, how many nighttime eating episodes occurred? If any, rate urge intensity for the episode on a 1–9 scale. | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Best-specific match: Nighttime uncontrolled eating corresponds to night eating with loss of control. The candidate leaf 26807 is 'loss_of_control_during_night_eating' within night eating syndrome / night eating disorder cluster (DSM-5TR). It aligns with the variable's focus on episodes with uncontrolled intake. Measurement context (EMA, past 6 hours, 4 prompts/day) fits the operational Night eating episodes/urge intensity structure captured by the item, providing a specific operational construct for uncontrolled nocturnal intake. Other candidates are broader (nocturnal eating episodes) or focus on awareness/distress rather than loss of control; hence 26807 is the most specific operational leaf.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 6 hours, how many nighttime eating episodes occurred? If any, rate urge intensity for the episode on a 1–9 scale.

criteria.label: Post-eating negative self-evaluation and physical disgust
criteria.query_text_used: Post-eating negative self-evaluation and physical disgust | Operational item/signal: Immediately after eating episodes, rate feelings of disgust or shame on a 1–9 scale. | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Post-eating negative self-evaluation and physical disgust' maps to after-eating affective response involving disgust and self-conscious emotions. Among candidate leaves, 'embarrassment_or_shame_about_binge_eating' (idx 26811) is the most specific operational construct that captures post-eating self-evaluation (shame/embarrassment) and the concomitant disgust reported in measurement (immediately after eating episodes, rating disgust or shame on a 1–9 scale). This aligns with C03 as a post-eating distress/self-evaluation mechanism; although focused on binge eating, it best matches the self-evaluative disgust construct in a momentary EMA context.

source_variable.label: Post-eating negative self-evaluation and physical disgust

measurement.item_or_signal: Immediately after eating episodes, rate feelings of disgust or shame on a 1–9 scale.

criteria.label: Rigid 'be good tomorrow' dieting intent
criteria.query_text_used: Rigid 'be good tomorrow' dieting intent | Operational item/signal: After an eating episode, rate your intention to restrict intake tomorrow on a 1–9 scale. | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Rigid 'be good tomorrow' dieting intent' indicates a cognitive dieting rule and plan to restrict intake tomorrow after an eating episode. The EMA signal ( After an eating episode, rate your intention to restrict intake tomorrow ) directly assesses future dietary restriction intent, a hallmark of dietary restriction/persistence of food rules. Among candidates, the closest operational leaf is 'dietary_restriction_or_food_rule_persistence' under ufed, which captures persistent dieting rules or restriction thoughts across time. This is more specific to the measured cognitive-dietary intent than broader patterns (e.g., rigid meal patterns) or distress-related items, aligning with C04 dieting pressures in the pseudoprofile context.

criteria.label: Nighttime food accessibility
criteria.query_text_used: Nighttime food accessibility | Operational item/signal: Today, rate how easy it was to access nighttime foods in your home on a 1–9 scale. | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Variable: Nighttime food accessibility; measurement: Today, rate how easy it was to access nighttime foods in your home (1–9). Among candidate leaves, the closest and most specific match is kitchensorbedsidefoodaccessduringepisodes (sleep-related eating disorder/night eating syndrome). This leaf explicitly encodes access to kitchen/bedside food during nocturnal episodes, directly operationalizing 'nighttime accessibility' as a mechanism enabling nocturnal consumption. It is under the Night Eating Syndrome / sleep-related eating disorder cluster, aligning with the pseudoprofile's focus on nocturnal eating patterns and access controls, and fits the EMA/self-report, momentary measurement design. Thus it best matches the intended variable without overfitting to craving or distress constructs.

criteria.label: Late-night wakefulness/opportunity
criteria.query_text_used: Late-night wakefulness/opportunity | Operational item/signal: During the past night around midnight, rate how awake you were on a 1–9 scale. | Polarity: unknown

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 is Late-night wakefulness/opportunity and the measurement is momentary EMA of wakefulness around midnight (1–9). The most specific CRITERION leaf matching nocturnal wakefulness during sleep is awakenings_during_sleep (leaf under circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders). This captures sleep interruptions at night, aligning with the measurement item and the context of nocturnal eating opportunities.

measurement.item_or_signal: During the past night around midnight, rate how awake you were on a 1–9 scale.

predictors.label: Sleep Regularization and Circadian Alignment
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep Regularization and Circadian Alignment | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Aligns sleep with circadian rhythm to reduce nocturnal wakefulness and hunger fluctuations. | Intervention examples: Set a consistent bed/wake time; limit evening light exposure; avoid caffeine after 6 pm. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, rate how consistent your sleep schedule was (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Circadian_Entrainment

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: Sleep circadian entrainment pathway with the specific leaf 'consistent_morning_anchor_routine' (consistent morning anchor routine). This directly maps to Sleep Regularization and Circadian Alignment by anchoring the sleep schedule to a stable morning routine, a key circadian entrainment cue. The measurement item assesses how consistent the sleep schedule was (1-9) via EMA, so a leaf emphasizing a consistent morning routine provides the most specific operational construct for P01 among the BIO circadian entrainment options, more so than broader leaves like general sleep diary or caffeine timing adjustments.

source_variable.label: Sleep Regularization and Circadian Alignment
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Aligns sleep with circadian rhythm to reduce nocturnal wakefulness and hunger fluctuations.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Set a consistent bed/wake time; limit evening light exposure; avoid caffeine after 6 pm.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate how consistent your sleep schedule was (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Regular sleep reduces daytime hunger variability.
Stabilized sleep reduces nocturnal eating episodes.
Better sleep may blunt post-eating negative affect.
Predictable schedule reduces tomorrow’s restriction pressure.
Circadian alignment lowers nighttime food accessibility cues.
Consistent sleep reduces late-night wakefulness.

predictors.label: Meal Planning and Structure
predictors.query_text_used: Meal Planning and Structure | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Structured meals support satiety, reduce hunger swings and nocturnal eating. | Intervention examples: Use meal templates; plan 3 meals + 1-2 planned snacks; grocery planning. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, rate how structured your meals were (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Nutrition_Hydration_and_Metabolic_Health / Meal_Planning_and_Structure

mapping.rationale: Variable P02 corresponds to Meal Planning and Structure. The most specific matching leaf is recipe_rotation_system under meal_planning_and_structure -> planning_tools. This directly represents a structured planning tool for meals (planning tools) and aligns with the measurement item 'Today, rate how structured your meals were (1-9)' by capturing the degree of meal structure via a rotation system. It is the most precise operational construct among candidates, better than snack-focused or broader template tools, and fits the pseudoprofile’s emphasis on a single, coherent domain per area.

source_variable.label: Meal Planning and Structure
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Structured meals support satiety, reduce hunger swings and nocturnal eating.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Use meal templates; plan 3 meals + 1-2 planned snacks; grocery planning.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate how structured your meals were (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Planned meals stabilize appetite signals.
Structured meals reduce nocturnal episodes.
Regular meals may lessen post-eating distress.
Meal plan supports flexible dieting rather than all-or-nothing rules.
Structured eating reduces nighttime access cues.
Better structure supports earlier sleep onset.

predictors.label: Mindful Eating Practice
predictors.query_text_used: Mindful Eating Practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Mindful awareness of hunger/fullness reduces impulsive intake and post-meal distress. | Intervention examples: Practice brief mindful eating cues; eat slowly; check-in with hunger signals. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Rate your level of mindful eating (attention to hunger/fullness) today on a 1–9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Third_Wave_and_Contextual_Therapies / Mindfulness_Based_Interventions

mapping.rationale: Variable Mindful Eating Practice matches P03 label directly. Measurement itemRate your level of mindful eating (attention to hunger/fullness) today on a 1–9 scale aligns with EMA_self_report 4 prompts/day. Ontology leaf: PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / informal_everyday_mindfulness / mindful_eating_practice. This provides a specific mindfulness-based, everyday practice construct that fits the intended predictor within a broad mindfulness context and aligns with the described measurement cadence and scale.

source_variable.label: Mindful Eating Practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Mindful awareness of hunger/fullness reduces impulsive intake and post-meal distress.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Practice brief mindful eating cues; eat slowly; check-in with hunger signals.

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your level of mindful eating (attention to hunger/fullness) today on a 1–9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Mindful eating reduces post-eating disgust and guilt.
Increased mindfulness improves appetite regulation.
Awareness can reduce nighttime intake urges.
Mindful approach supports flexible restraint.
Mindfulness reduces cue-driven access to nighttime foods.
Calm awareness may improve sleep onset.

predictors.label: Environmental Nighttime Access Reduction
predictors.query_text_used: Environmental Nighttime Access Reduction | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Limiting nighttime food accessibility reduces cues and temptation for late eating. | Intervention examples: Remove easy nighttime snacks; use containerized snacks; keep kitchen door closed. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, rate how easy it was to access nighttime foods in your home (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Environmental_Exposures_and_Built_Environment_Optimization / Nighttime_Access_Control

mapping.rationale: Variable P04 targets reducing access to nighttime foods via the home environment. The EMA item directly assesses 'ease of access' to nighttime foods, reflecting environmental cues/availability. The closest, most specific ontology leaf is BIO / nutrition_hydration_and_metabolic_health / eating_behavior_and_satiety_skills / environment_design_for_eating, which encapsulates modifying the eating environment to influence intake and access at night. Other candidates focus on lighting or noise rather than food access, making environment_design_for_eating the best fit for the intended mechanism.

source_variable.label: Environmental Nighttime Access Reduction
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Limiting nighttime food accessibility reduces cues and temptation for late eating.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Remove easy nighttime snacks; use containerized snacks; keep kitchen door closed.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate how easy it was to access nighttime foods in your home (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Reduced access lowers nocturnal eating frequency.
Lower accessibility cues decrease nighttime intake.
Fewer opportunities support better sleep.
Reduced cues may stabilize appetite swings.
Structure and reduced temptations lessen tomorrow’s restriction pressure.
Less nighttime exposure reduces post-eating distress triggers.

predictors.label: Pre-Sleep Wind-Down and Screen Time Constraints
predictors.query_text_used: Pre-Sleep Wind-Down and Screen Time Constraints | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: A calm pre-sleep window reduces sleep fragmentation and late-night wakefulness, indirectly moderating appetite cycles. | Intervention examples: Limit screen time 60 minutes before bed; read a non-stimulating book; off-device winding-down activities. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, rate your pre-sleep wind-down quality (1-9) and screen exposure (0-1) in the last hour. | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices / Presleep_downshift

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is presleep_detachment_routine_design_psychological (PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / presleep_mental_skills / detachment_and_unloading). The variable P05 represents a psychological wind-down routine before sleep and constraints on screen time, aligning with presleep cognitive-emotional downregulation strategies. The measurement items (pre-sleep wind-down quality; last-hour screen exposure) map to cognitive/behavioral detachment and unloading processes intended to reduce pre-sleep arousal, which this leaf explicitly targets. It is the most specific PSYCHO node within Presleep_downshift that captures a structured, cognitive-behavioral wind-down design, consistent with the pseudoprofile's domain organization.

source_variable.label: Pre-Sleep Wind-Down and Screen Time Constraints
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: A calm pre-sleep window reduces sleep fragmentation and late-night wakefulness, indirectly moderating appetite cycles.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Limit screen time 60 minutes before bed; read a non-stimulating book; off-device winding-down activities.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, rate your pre-sleep wind-down quality (1-9) and screen exposure (0-1) in the last hour.

expected_effects.rationale: Better pre-sleep routine supports earlier and more stable sleep.
Reduced late-night eating opportunities via wind-down effects.
Stabilized hunger cues through consistent evening routines.
Less nighttime accessibility cues during wind-down.
Less promise-like restriction pressure due to calmer evenings.
Reduced arousal may blunt post-eating distress.

predictors.label: Self-Monitoring and Appetite/Satiety Logs
predictors.query_text_used: Self-Monitoring and Appetite/Satiety Logs | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Frequent logging of appetite and satiety yields within-person variance and detects changes linked to C01–C06. | Intervention examples: Simple appetite/satiety diary entries; timestamped notes on meals. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Rate current appetite and satiety on a 1–9 scale; optionally log last meal size (small/medium/large). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Personalization_Monitoring_and_Data_Feedback / Self_Assessment_Logs / Somatic_and_Interoceptive_Selfreports

mapping.rationale: P06 maps to Self-Monitoring and Appetite/Satiety Logs. The best-matching leaf is appetite_and_satiety_log (idx 4511) under BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / somatic_and_interoceptive_selfreports. This leaf directly corresponds to logging appetite and satiety, which aligns with the variable label, and the item/mode details match: measurement.item_or_signal = 'Rate current appetite and satiety on a 1–9 scale; optionally log last meal size'; measurement.mode = EMA_self_report; measurement.channel = Smartphone EMA app; measurement.assessment_type = momentary; recall_window aligns with 'since last_prompt'. The mechanism rationale (frequent logging yields within-person variance to detect changes related to C01–C06) also fits. This leaf is the most specific operational construct for P06 and matches the intended data-collection design (4 EMA prompts/day).

source_variable.label: Self-Monitoring and Appetite/Satiety Logs
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Frequent logging of appetite and satiety yields within-person variance and detects changes linked to C01–C06.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Simple appetite/satiety diary entries; timestamped notes on meals.

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate current appetite and satiety on a 1–9 scale; optionally log last meal size (small/medium/large).

expected_effects.rationale: Regular logging improves self-awareness of hunger fluctuations.
More complete episode accounting supports detection of nocturnal eating patterns.
Immediate logging may reduce rumination about post-eating distress.
Feedback loops help adjust tomorrow’s intake planning.
Reflective logging reduces cue-driven cravings.
Logs enable detection of sleep–hunger coupling.
